one_line_takeaway: A first end-to-end security measurement of the MCP ecosystem showing that honest-but-flawed hosts and registries let adversarial servers and malicious tool metadata drive unauthorized LLM-invoked operations at scale, with a pre-integration screening tool (MCPInspect) that flags 833 vulnerable and 18 suspicious servers among 67,057.
key_finding: Hosts blindly trust LLM tool selections and server-supplied metadata. Cursor is vulnerable to tool confusion (always invokes the first-listed tool when two servers share a tool name, ignoring its own mcp/server-name prefixes). No host verifies that an LLM-returned tool still exists (context-dangling tool). Tool poisoning and tool shadowing succeed across hosts, driven mainly by LLM behavior: Claude Sonnet 4 and Gemini 2.5 Pro follow malicious descriptions/error messages (Gemini even recognizes anomalies but proceeds), while GPT-4o is more conservative; Claude Desktop is the only host doing any parameter validation, though enforcement is inconsistent. At the registry level, weak vetting exposes valid GitHub tokens, 212 re-registrable (hijackable) accounts and 304 redirection-hijackable accounts across decentralized registries, and 408 affix-squatting groups on npm (80.6% cross-maintainer). MCPInspect flagged 833 vulnerable servers and 18 servers with suspicious tool descriptions across 67,057 collected servers.
core_contribution: The first cross-entity security study of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, organized around a unified two-stage attack surface: (i) registry-level attacks, where weak vetting and ownership checks let adversarial or hijacked servers enter hosts, and (ii) post-integration attacks, where attacker-controlled tool metadata (not code) steers LLM tool selection and parameters that hosts invoke without independent verification. The authors newly name and characterize several concrete issues (tool confusion, context-dangling tool, tool poisoning, tool shadowing, maintainer/redirection hijacking, affix-squatting) and build MCPInspect, a pre-integration screening tool that flags misleading tool metadata and exploitable code vulnerabilities before a server is integrated. This is the paper's own work; prior blog/academic findings (Invariant Labs tool poisoning, WhatsApp MCP exfiltration, Shi et al. on adversarial tool descriptions, Gu et al. on registry account reclamation) are cited as Related Work and explicitly contrasted, not claimed.
threat_model: Registries and hosts are honest-but-flawed (not malicious, but lacking validation/enforcement); LLMs are uncompromised external APIs; host-server-LLM communication is secure. The adversary controls or hijacks MCP servers and controls only its own server's tool metadata, with no control over the host, other servers, or the backbone LLM. Two-stage attack surface: Stage-I places an adversarial server into a public registry for normal discovery/integration (via leaked GitHub tokens, reclaiming deleted/redirected server links, or affix-squatting names). Stage-II uses attacker-controlled metadata (descriptions, crafted error messages) to induce attacker-intended operations that the host invokes without verifying tool identity or parameters. Code-level vulnerabilities are not required but amplify impact when present. Host features (auto-updates, auto-run, collapsed invocation display) further amplify Stage-II.
limitations: Feasibility trials use only 5 runs per host-model pair and the authors explicitly disclaim broad statistical ASR claims. Tool extraction misses tools added dynamically (e.g., in a for loop). The vulnerability generator uses default Semgrep rules, yielding false positives (manual audit of 41/377 mcp.so servers found 4 FPs, i.e., 90.24% precision); MCP-specific custom rules are future work. Manual metadata verification covers small samples (6 suspicious mcp.so descriptions; most are proof-of-concept/deliberately-vulnerable servers such as damn-vulnerable-MCP-server). Data is a single-snapshot crawl (late June-early July 2025); on re-collection only one expired token remained. Disclosure impact was limited: Cursor classified tool confusion as a functional issue and mcp.so did not respond.

datasets_used: 67,057 MCP servers collected late June-early July 2025 across six registries
Four decentralized registries: mcp.so, MCP Market, MCP Store (>20,000 servers, largest), Pulse MCP
Two centralized registries: Smithery and npm (each <8,000 servers)
52,102/52,539 (99.16%) decentralized-registry servers hosted on GitHub public repos
5,659 mcp.so server configurations scanned for leaked credentials
Manually constructed dataset to evaluate MCPInspect accuracy; 41 of 377 mcp.so vulnerable servers manually audited
quantitative_results: MCPInspect flagged 833 vulnerable servers and 18 servers with suspicious tool descriptions overall
Vulnerable servers per registry: mcp.so 377 (4.44%, highest), Pulse MCP 157, MCP Store 151 (1.2%, lowest), MCP Market 148
Tools extracted per registry: MCP Store 12,541, mcp.so 8,484, MCP Market 6,262, Pulse MCP 3,771
Vulnerable-server detection precision 90.24% (4 false positives in 41 audited mcp.so servers)
Credential leakage: 9 GitHub tokens found in 5,659 mcp.so configs; 5 still valid as of July 2025 (only 1 expired token on re-collection)
Maintainer hijacking: 212 re-registrable accounts (15.37% of 1,379 invalid links); mcp.so 111, MCP Market 95, MCP Store 5, Pulse MCP 1
Redirection hijacking: 304 reclaimable accounts (MCP Store 155, mcp.so 98, MCP Market 50, Pulse MCP 1); 139 servers still actively re-submitted under renamed accounts
Affix-squatting: 408 npm groups with identical names but different affixes, 329 (80.6%) cross-maintainer; affixes -mcp (166), -mcp-server (138), mcp- (88), mcp-package-server (33)
Incomplete info rates (Table III): mcp.so 6.75% invalid links; MCP Store 4.25% empty content and 10.11% missing README

Tool poisoning ASR (Table II) highest for built-in tools and crafted error messages; Claude Sonnet 4 and Gemini 2.5 Pro often 100%, GPT-4o lower (e.g., Cursor & GPT-4o crafted description built-in 80, server-provided 60); Cline & Gemini 2.5 Pro reached 100 across all six columns
Suspicious metadata verification: of 6 mcp.so cases, 4 proof-of-concept, 2 tool-confusion; five suspicious servers unaware of tool confusion directly invoked other tools within descriptions
baselines_compared: Measurement/systematization study rather than an ML-benchmark comparison; no accuracy baseline models are compared

MCPInspect components lean on existing tools: Semgrep (static vulnerability detection, default Python/JS/TS + security-audit + secret rules), gitleaks (credential detection), and the GitHub User/API for account-availability checks

Positioned against prior work by contrast: Invariant Labs tool poisoning/WhatsApp MCP exfiltration blogs, Shi et al. (adversarial tool-description bias, benchmark setup), Wang et al. (multi-source input manipulation), Zou et al. (RAG corpus poisoning), and Gu et al. (registry account reclamation)
